perturbation.original_text: 5.  EXCEPTIONS This Agreement does not apply to Confidential Information: 5.1 to the extent that the Confidential Information is or comes into the public domain other than by breach of this Agreement; 5.2 which the Receiving Party can show by its written or other records was in its possession prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party and which had not previously been obtained from the Disclosing Party or another person under an obligation of confiden tiality ; 5.3 which the Receiving Party obtains or has avai lable from a source other than the Disclosing Party without breaching any obligation of confiden tiality ; 5.4 which has been disclosed by the Disclosing Party to another person without an obligation of confiden tiality .
perturbation.changed_text: 5.  EXCEPTIONS This Agreement does not apply to Confidential Information: 5.1 to the extent that the Confidential Information is or comes into the public domain; 5.2 which the Receiving Party can show by its written or other records was in its possession prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party; 5.3 which the Receiving Party obtains or has avai lable from a source other than the Disclosing Party; 5.4 which has been disclosed by the Disclosing Party to another person.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clauses 'other than by breach of this Agreement', 'and which had not previously been obtained from the Disclosing Party or another person under an obligation of confiden tiality', 'without breaching any obligation of confiden tiality', and 'without an obligation of confiden tiality' from Section 5 creates an in-text contradiction. Now, even if the Receiving Party obtains Confidential Information from the Disclosing Party under an obligation of confidentiality but it's been disclosed to another person without an obligation of confidentiality, it's no longer protected. This weakens the protection afforded to the Disclosing Party, specifically contradicting section 2.1, to keep the other party's Confidential Information confidential, the contradictory implications now reside in the obligation outlined in Clause 3, regarding the measures for keeping confidential information confidential.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. KEEPING CONFIDENT IAL INFORMATION C ONFIDENTIAL Each party shall in relation to the Confidential Information of the other party exercise no lesser security measures and degree of care than those which it applies to its own confidential information and which it warrants as providing adequate protection against any unauthorised disclosure, copying or use.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 5 weakens the protection of Confidential Information by removing key clauses that previously ensured confidentiality was maintained even if the information entered the public domain or was disclosed to another party. This directly contradicts Section 3, which mandates that each party must exercise no lesser security measures and degree of care than those applied to their own confidential information. The removal of clauses in Section 5 allows for scenarios where information initially received under an obligation of confidentiality can lose that protection, undermining the security measures required by Section 3. Therefore, the changed text causes a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 8. TERM AND TERMINATION 8.1 This Agreement shall come into effect (i) until parties sign the underlying documentation in relation to the Project, or (ii) in the event parties do not sign any such documentation , on the date of this Agreement and shall continue to be in full force and effect for a period of 2 years.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. TERM AND TERMINATION 8.1 This Agreement shall come into effect (i) until parties sign the underlying documentation in relation to the Project, or (ii) in the event parties do not sign any such documentation , on the date of this Agreement and shall continue to be in full force and effect indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the term from 'a period of 2 years' to 'indefinitely' creates a direct contradiction. Initially, the agreement had a defined duration. Now, it purports to last forever. This directly impacts Section 8.2 which details actions to be taken 'within 14 days of receipt of a wri tten request of the other party' after termination. If the agreement never terminates, these actions are never triggered, creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8.2 Each party undertakes within 14 days of receipt of a wri tten request of the other party: 8.2.1 subject to Clause 8.3, to return to the other party all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control that contain any part of the other party's Confidential Information; or 8.2.2 subject to Clause 8.3, to de stroy by shredding or incineration all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control which reflect or have - 4 - been generated from any part of the other party's Confidential Information and to certify to the other party that this has been done.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Section 8.1 to make the agreement last 'indefinitely' instead of for 'a period of 2 years'. This directly contradicts Section 8.2, which outlines actions to be taken 'within 14 days of receipt of a written request of the other party' after termination. If the agreement lasts indefinitely, there is no termination, and therefore no trigger for the actions described in Section 8.2, creating a strong in-text contradiction as the obligation in 8.2 can never be fulfilled.

perturbation.original_text: 9. ANNOUNCEMENTS 9.1 Neither party shall disclose or announce the Purpose to another person without the prior written consent of the other party (such consent not to be unreasonably withheld or delayed) except to the extent that such disclosure or announcement is required as specified in Clause 9.2 9.2 To the extent that a disclosure or announcement of the type referred to in Clause 9.1 is required by law, by a rule of a stock exchange on which the party's shares a re listed or traded or by a governmental authority or other authority with relevant powers, the disclosure or announcement shall so far as is practicable be made after consultation with the other party and after taking into account the other party's reason able requirements as to its timing, content and manner of making or despatch.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. ANNOUNCEMENTS 9.1 Either party may disclose or announce the Purpose to another person without any prior written consent of the other party, except to the extent that such disclosure or announcement is required as specified in Clause 9.2 9.2 To the extent that a disclosure or announcement of the type referred to in Clause 9.1 is required by law, by a rule of a stock exchange on which the party's shares a re listed or traded or by a governmental authority or other authority with relevant powers, the disclosure or announcement shall so far as is practicable be made after consultation with the other party and after taking into account the other party's reason able requirements as to its timing, content and manner of making or despatch.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Neither party shall disclose' to 'Either party may disclose' directly contradicts the initial requirement for prior written consent. Now, parties can freely disclose the Purpose, except when legal requirements dictate a different process outlined in 9.2. This affects section 2.3, because now there is an in-text contradiction, where previously it was not possible to disclose the Purpose, and now, by inserting the perturbation in section 9.1, that created an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.3 not to disclose the other party's Confidential Information to another person and to use all reasonable efforts to prevent any such disclosure exc ept as permitted under Clause 4 and Clause 7.
perturbation.justification: The original text in section 9.1 stated 'Neither party shall disclose' requiring prior written consent. The changed text modifies this to 'Either party may disclose' removing the requirement for prior written consent. This directly contradicts section 2.3 which states 'not to disclose the other party's Confidential Information to another person and to use all reasonable efforts to prevent any such disclosure'. The modification in 9.1 allows disclosure, while 2.3 prohibits it, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 10.3 A person who is not a party to this Agreemen t has no right under section 6:253 of the Dutch Civil Code to enforce or to enjoy the benefit of any term of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.3 A person who is not a party to this Agreemen t has the right under section 6:253 of the Dutch Civil Code to enforce or to enjoy the benefit of any term of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Switching 'no right' to 'the right' regarding third-party enforcement under Dutch law creates a direct contradiction. Previously, only the signing parties could enforce the agreement. Now, a third party can. The impact of this change affects all the sections related to Confidential Information and its use. More specifically section 3, regarding keeping confidential information, now is being contradicted, because a 3rd party can enforce this section.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. KEEPING CONFIDENT IAL INFORMATION C ONFIDENTIAL Each party shall in relation to the Confidential Information of the other party exercise no lesser security measures and degree of care than those which it applies to its own confidential information and which it warrants as providing adequate protection against any unauthorised disclosure, copying or use.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly denies third parties the right to enforce the agreement under Dutch law. The changed text directly reverses this, granting them that right. This directly contradicts section 3, which outlines the confidentiality obligations of 'each party'. If a third party can now enforce the agreement, they can enforce section 3, which contradicts the original intent that only the original signing parties could enforce this section.
